coredata.dc:title: In situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with enhanced interface connection via calcination of the precursors for photocatalytic H2 evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ke
Wang, Xuezhao
Pan, Hui
Liu, Yingliang
Xu, Shengang
Cao, Shaokui
coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon dots (CDs)/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) hybrids were fabricated via an in situ thermal polymerization of the precursors, urea and glucose. This heterojunction catalyst exhibited enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity under visible-light (λ > 420). A sample of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids, CN/G0.5, which was prepared from 0.5 mg of glucose in 6.0 g of urea (8.3 × 10−3 wt% glucose), exhibited the best photocatalytic performance for hydrogen production from water under visible light irradiation, which is about 4.55 times of that of the bulk g-C3N4 (BCN). The improvement of photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the construction of built-in electric field at the interface of CDs and g-C3N4, which could improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pair. Moreover, the tight connection of CDs with g-C3N4 would serve as a well electron transport channel, which could promote the photocatalytic H2 evolution ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids
Urea
Glucose
In situ fabrication
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photoelectrochemical and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of BCN, CN/G0.25, CN/G0.5, CN/G1.0 and CN/C0.5.
TEM images of (A) BCN, (B) CN/G0.25, (C) CN/G0.5, (D) CN/G1.0 and (E) CN/C0.5.
FT-IR spectra of (A) BCN, (B) CN/G0.25, (C) CN/G0.5, (D) CN/G1.0 and(E) CN/C0.5.
XPS spectra of BCN and CN/G0.5: C1s (A) and N1s (B).
UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (A) BCN and CN/Gx (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0) and (B) CN/G0.5 and CN/C0.5.
The PL spectra of BCN, CN/Gx (x = 0.25, 0.5, and 1.0) and CN/C0.5.
Photocatalytic H2 evolution of BCN and CN/Gx (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 and 5.0).
(A) Photocatalytic H2 evolution of CN/G0.5, CN/C0.5 and (B) BCN, CN/G0.5, BCN/8h and CN/G0.5/8h.
Time course of H2 evolution for CN/G0.5.
The photocurrent response of BCN, CN/Gx (x = 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0) and CN/C0.5.
The proposed mechanism for the synthesis and photocatalytic process of CN/Gx hybrids.

head.ce:title: In situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with enhanced interface connection via calcination of the precursors for photocatalytic H2 evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon dots (CDs)/graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) hybrids were fabricated via an in situ thermal polymerization of the precursors, urea and glucose. This heterojunction catalyst exhibited enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity under visible-light (λ > 420). A sample of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids, CN/G0.5, which was prepared from 0.5 mg of glucose in 6.0 g of urea (8.3 × 10−3 wt% glucose), exhibited the best photocatalytic performance for hydrogen production from water under visible light irradiation, which is about 4.55 times of that of the bulk g-C3N4 (BCN). The improvement of photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the construction of built-in electric field at the interface of CDs and g-C3N4, which could improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pair. Moreover, the tight connection of CDs with g-C3N4 would serve as a well electron transport channel, which could promote the photocatalytic H2 evolution ability.
CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid photocatalyst with close interface connection was prepared via calcinating the precursors (urea and glucose) for photocatalytic H2 evolution from water.

ce:section.ce:para: Focusing on the energy shortage and environmental pollution, hydrogen produced by water splitting under solar irradiation is becoming an attractive pathway as a clean and renewable energy resource [1–3]. An n-type TiO2 single-crystal electrode for photocatalytic water splitting into H2 and O2 under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation was firstly reported by Fujishima and Honda in 1972 [4]. Since that, various metallic compounds-based semiconductors, such as ZnO [5], perovskite [6] and CdS [7], have been proved as promising photocatalysts for water splitting. However, their widespread application is greatly restricted by their high cost, poor stability and toxicity.

In recent years, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymeric semiconductor with a typical two-dimensional structure, has received much attention for applications in photocatalytic water splitting [8–11], CO2 reduction [12,13] and pollutant degradation [14,15], due to its high thermal and chemical stabilities and an appropriate band gap (2.7 eV). However, the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 is greatly hindered by the rapid recombination of photo-generated charge carriers and the inefficient utilization for visible-light [16]. Various strategies have been proposed to improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, such as doping [17–19], exfoliation [20], morphology control [21], preparation [22] and effective construction of surface heterojunctions [23–25]. It has been proved that an appropriate band structure could be constructed by the effective construction of surface heterojunctions, e.g. g-C3N4/TiO2 [23,26], g-C3N4/ZnO [27] and g-C3N4/BiOI [28,29], through which the light absorption could be enhanced and the separation of electron-hole pairs could be promoted.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new family member of carbon-based nanomaterials, have drawn a great attention because of their unique electronic properties [30,31]. Very recently, Kang et al. reported carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/g-C3N4 composites prepared by calcinating urea and ammoniated CQDs at 550 °C for overall water splitting under visible light irradiation [32]. Tang et al. also reported that the CQDs/g-C3N4 composites fabricated by using g-C3N4 nanosheets and CQDs with a hydrothermal method could respond to near-infrared light to produce hydrogen from water [33]. As far as we know, these are the two typical strategies for the fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 composites, i.e. the direct blending of the as-prepared CDs and g-C3N4 by a hydrothermal method [34–37] and the calcination of the as-prepared CDs with a g-C3N4 precursor [12,32,38,39]. It is noted that the formation of effective heterojunctions is highly depended on the interfacial contacts of the two components.

Herein, we report for the first time the in situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids by calcinating their precursors, urea and glucose, at 550 °C. The two precursors were mixed in a homogeneous aqueous solution, thus the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of glucose and the amino groups of urea, could make a uniform distribution of glucose in the urea matrix. Hence, in the following steps, the recrystallization and calcination of glucose mixed urea, could yield CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with enhanced interfacial connections and finally an even distribution of CDs in the g-C3N4 matrix. The obtained hybrids would have enhanced interfacial connections between g-C3N4 and CDs, prohibited agglomeration and burning of CDs during calcination. Thus, the charge transfer would be promoted and the recombination of electrons and holes would be prevented. As expected, such prepared CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids exhibited greatly improved photocatalytic activity as well as stability for hydrogen evolution from water as compared with the bulk g-C3N4 (BCN).
All the chemicals were of analytical purity and were used as received.

BCN was prepared by thermal polymerization of urea in a semi-closed system according to literature procedures [40, 41]. Typically, 6 g of urea was put into a 50 mL crucible. And then the crucible was wrapped with two layers of aluminum foil, heated to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C min−1 in a tube furnace. Kept at this temperature for 2 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The sample was milled into powders in an agate mortar. This resultant bulk g-C3N4 was marked as BCN.

CN/Gx hybrids were constructed by similar procedures as those of BCN, and the flow chart is shown in Fig. S1†. In a typical case, 6 g of urea and different contents of glucose (0, 0.25 mg (4.7 × 10−3 wt%), 0.5 mg (8.3 × 10−3 wt%), 1.0 mg (1.7 × 10−2 wt%) and 5.0 mg (8.3 × 10−2 wt%)) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL crucible, then transferred to a drying oven and kept at 80 °C until most of the water was evaporated. Then the crucible was taken out of the oven and was allowed to be gradually cooled down to room temperature, stacked needle-like urea containing glucose crystals were obtained. Succeeding thermal polymerization yielded CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with different CDs contents, which were marked as CN/Gx (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 and 5.0), x represents the milligrams of glucose charged in 6 g of urea.

For comparison, a sample of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids, CN/C0.5, in which the chemical composition is same as that of CN/G0.5, was prepared. The only difference is that the CDs were firstly prepared and then mixed with the urea aqueous solution. Succeeding procedures were the same as those of CN/Gx preparation. Firstly, 40 mg of glucose was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h [42]. After cooling to room temperature, CDs were obtained as a brown solution and was collected into a beaker. Then, 0.5 mL of the as-prepared CDs solution was directly mixed with 6 g of urea in 10 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL crucible to prepare the precursor of CN/C0.5 and then calcinated as those of CN/Gx preparation.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on Rigaku Ultima IV using Cu irradiation from 5° to 80°. Morphologies of the hybrids were examined by using a Tecnai G2 20 200 kV transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained using a Bruker tensor II spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS instrument using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation as the X-ray source. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded on an Agilent Cary 5000. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) were detected with a PTI QuantaMaster 40 phosphorescence/fluorescence spectrofluorometer excited by incident light of 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on a RST5000 electrochemical workstation (Suzhou Risetest Instrument Co., Ltd, China) using a conventional three-electrode cell with a Pt sheet and a saturated calomel electrode as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The visible light was generated by a 300 W Xe arc lamp with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). The electrolyte was 0.1 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution. The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 0.25 mg of the as-prepared hybrids sample was suspended in 0.5 mL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to get a turbid liquid and 5 μL of Nafion 117 solution was added as a binder to avoid the dropping of the sample. Then the suspension was dip-coated on a piece of 20 mm × 20 mm indium-tin oxide (ITO) glass at 80 °C in a drying oven. After drying, in order to get the same active area of samples for different electrodes, a blade was used to remove the edge of samples, and a 20 mm × 15 mm area coated by samples was obtained. Before the photocurrent measurements, the working electrode was immersed in the electrolyte for 60 s to get stable currents. In addition, the electrodes were back-illuminated to minimize the influence of the thickness of the semiconductor layer. The transient photocurrent density (TPD) was measured under chopped light irritation (light on-off cycles: 50 s) at a fixed potential of −0.4 V vs. SCE (Saturated Calomel Electrode). LSV tests were performed by using a Chi600E electrochemical workshop (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in the range of −0.4 to 0.3 V vs. RHE (Reversible Hydrogen Electrode) at room temperature.

H2 evolution of the as-prepared photocatalysts were tested by using Labsolra-III Al fully automated photocatalytic system (Perfectlight company, Beijing, China), and a 300 W Xe arc lamp with a UV cut-off filter (λ > 420) nm was used as a light source. The reaction was performed by dispersing 50 mg of the photocatalyst sample into an aqueous solution (100 mL) containing triethanolamine (20 mL) as a sacrificial electron donor, and the reaction temperature was controlled at 7 °C by a cooling water flow. Then 3 wt% Pt was loaded on the surface samples by in situ photodeposition method using H2PtCl6·6H2O as a starting material. The amount of produced hydrogen was analyzed by using gas chromatograph (GC7900, TianMei, China) equipped with a thermal conductive detector (TCD) and a capillary column (5 Å molecular sieve). High purity nitrogen was used as the carrier gas.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of BCN, CN/G0.25, CN/G0.5, CN/G1.0 and CN/C0.5. The diffraction peaks of BCN were observed at 13.1° and 27.6°, which can be indexed to the (100) and (002) diffraction planes of g-C3N4 [40]. The main peaks of CN/G0.5 and CN/C0.5 are similar with those of BCN. Somewhat differently, the intensity of the (002) peak, which shifted from 27.6° to 27.8°, demonstrating the reduction in the stacking distance of the layer structure from 0.322 nm to 0.318 nm. The reduced distance between the layers can be ascribed to the effect of CDs on the thermal polymerization of urea. Moreover, no CDs diffraction peaks could be observed, which was attributed to the small amount of CDs loading on the surface of g-C3N4 below the detectable limits of XRD instrument [43].

The microscopic morphologies of BCN, CN/G0.25, CN/G0.5, CN/G1.0 and CN/C0.5 were characterized by TEM and are shown in Fig. 2 . And the TEM image of CDs is shown in Fig. S2†, which indicates the diameter of CDs is about 4 nm. The TEM image of BCN in Fig. 2A shows a typical two-dimensional accumulated layered structure of g-C3N4. Fig. 2B–D shows the morphologies of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids, and it can be observed that the amount of CDs on the surface of g-C3N4 increased with the increase of glucose amount added in the precursors. Particularly, the size of CDs on the surface of g-C3N4 in the CN/G0.5 sample, shown in Fig. 2C, is about 23 nm. In Fig. S2†, the TEM images of CN/G0.5 are also provided, and the CDs shows well distribution on the surface of g-C3N4. However, an obvious tubular accumulation of CDs was observed on the surface of g-C3N4 as shown in Fig. 2D, revealing the agglomeration of CDs during the thermal polymerization of the precursors with excessive glucose used. Moreover, the CDs on the surface of CN/G0.5 (Fig. 2C) showed a more uniform distribution than those of CN/C0.5 (Fig. 2E) which was prepared by the calcination of the as-prepared CDs with a g-C3N4 precursor. This result strongly proved that the in situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids by calcinating their precursors, urea and glucose, could restrain the agglomeration of CDs on the g-C3N4 surface at an appropriate composition.

Fig. 3 shows the FI-IR spectra of the as-prepared samples. The FI-IR spectrum of BCN is similar with those reported in literature [34,40]. Specifically, the absorptions from 1800 to 1400 cm−1 are the stretching vibration modes of heptazine-derived repeating units. The intense peak at 814.7 cm−1 is considered as the bending vibration characteristic of triazine cycles. And the peaks at 1323.6 and 1240.9 cm−1 are corresponded to the stretching vibration of connected units of C N (-C)C (full condensation) or C NHC (partial condensation). As for the broad bands in the range of 3500 and 3000 cm−1 belong to the terminal amino groups (NH2 or NH–groups). The FI-IR spectra of the remaining samples are basically similar with that of BCN, while the corresponding absorptions gradually weakened as the increase in CDs content, which might be taken as an indicator that CDs have been coupled with g-C3N4 successfully [34].

As shown in Fig. S3†, the XPS survey spectra revealed that both BCN and CN/G0.5 are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and a small amount of oxygen. The C1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three species (Fig. 4 A): the sp2 C C bond at 284.6 eV, the CN bond at ca. 286.1 eV and the sp2 N CN at ca. 288.0 eV. An additional peak emerged at 288.6 eV in the spectrum of CN/G0.5 could be attributed to the carboxyl groups (COOH) in CDs, suggested the successful incorporation of CDs on g-C3N4 [12]. The N1s spectra can be deconvoluted into four peaks (Fig. 4B): the sp2-hybridized N (C NC) at ca. 398.5 eV, the tertiary N in tri-s-triazine units (N-(C)3) at ca. 399.9 eV, the amino N H bonds at 401.2 eV and the positively charged g-C3N4 heterocycles at ca. 404.1 eV [44]. It could be also observed that almost all of the C1s and N1s peaks in the XPS spectra of CN/G0.5 showed a more or less slight shift as compared with those of BCN, suggested that the coupling interactions exist between CDs and g-C3N4. And these interactions gave rise to a high-quality contact interface, and further promoted the transportation of charge carriers.

Fig. 5 A shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of BCN and CN/Gx (x = 0, 0.25, 0.5 and 1.0). BCN exhibited an intrinsic absorption edge at 440 nm with a calculated band gap of 2.8 eV [32, 40]. After coupling with CDs, there was no significant shift of the absorption edge relative to that of BCN. In addition, with the CDs content increasing, the visible and UV light absorption intensity of the sample increased, which could improve the utilization of light. And this absorption increasing was illustrated by the color variation from yellow to brown [12], which was shown in the inset of Fig. 5A. However, overmuch CDs in the photocatalyst cause an excess of coverage on the surface of g-C3N4, which would affect the light absorption of g-C3N4. Moreover, comparing the absorption spectrum of CN/G0.5 with CN/C0.5 (Fig. 5B), there is no obvious difference. And according to the Kubelka-Munk function, they have the same bang gap (2.63 eV). Therefore, the main factors of the difference in photocatalytic performance of CN/G0.5 and CN/C0.5 would not be the absorption of photocatalysts.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the as-prepared samples were obtained for studying the charge separation [34, 45], as shown in Fig. 6 . For the CN/Gx hybrids with different proportion of CDs, all of them show reduced PL intensity as compared with BCN, indicating that the coupling with CDs can suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carries. And the PL intensity of CN/Gx firstly reduced with the increase in CDs content up to CN/G0.5, and then increased with the further increase in CDs content. It revealed that CN/G0.5 showed the lowest PL intensity, which suggested the weakest recombination of electrons and holes. More significantly, the PL intensity of CN/G0.5 is much lower than that of CN/C0.5, implying the interfacial interactions between CDs and g-C3N4 in CN/G0.5 could greatly suppress the charge recombination.

The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared samples were evaluated by H2 production reaction. H2 evolution rate (HER) of all samples were shown in Fig. S4†. As shown in Fig. 7 , in general, all samples showed stable photocatalytic activity for hydrogen production. Particularly, CN/G0 showed the same HER as BCN, indicating that the recrystallization of urea have no obvious influence on the photocatalytic H2 evolution of g-C3N4. The highest HER was observed with CN/G0.5, which is about 4.55 folds of that of BCN. These promotions could be attributed to the higher separation of electron-hole pairs and restrain the recombination of electrons and holes as revealed through the PL results.

To further investigate different kinds of carbon source whether affect the photocatalytic H2 evolution, using CDs to replace glucose, a sample CN/C0.5 was prepared by similar procedures as those of CN/G0.5. The H2 evolution of these two samples was shown in Fig. 8 A. As expected, the HER of CN/C0.5 was lower than that of CN/G0.5, which meant, during thermal polymerization, directly using glucose as the precursor of CDs is better for the final photocatalytic activity. It is because that the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of glucose and the amino groups of urea could enhance the interfacial connections of CN/G0.5, which could prevent the agglomeration of CDs, charge transfer would be promoted and the recombination of electrons and holes would be prevented.

Besides, to explore the importance of the protection of NH3, two samples CN/G0.5/8h and BCN/8h, which were calcinated for 8 h rather than 2 h, was prepared. As shown in Fig. 8B, the HER of BCN/8h is higher than that of BCN, which explains enough calcination can develop the HER of BCN [41]. However, comparing with CN/G0.5, the HER of CN/G0.5/8h decreased. This was attributed to the amount of the shielding gas (NH3) gradually decreased, which result in the burning of CDs with the time increasing. Moreover, melamine as precursor to couple with CDs by directly thermal polymerization also have mentioned the important protection of NH3 [39]. Different with melamine, urea as the precursor can release more NH3, which can further restrain the burning and agglomeration of CDs.

To verify the stability of the photocatalyst, the time course of H2 release over CN/G0.5 has been carried out (Fig. 9 ), and each cycle include 4 h continuous illumination. After 4 cycles H2 evolution experiments, the HER did not exhibit obvious reduction, suggesting the good stability of CN/G0.5, which may be attributed to the tight connection between CDs and g-C3N4.

The transient photocurrent responses were tested under visible light irradiation to investigate the photo-induced electron-hole transfer and separation. As shown in Fig. 10 , as expected, all samples exhibited higher photocurrent density than BCN, and it is clearly observed that CN/G0.5 exhibited the highest photocurrent density among all samples. This result was well attributed to the improvement of photo-induced charge transfer and separation, and suggested the best photocatalytic performance, which were in accordance with the result of H2 evolution. Furthermore, CN/G0.5 showed better stability of photocurrent than that of CN/C0.5, which agrees with that using glucose as the precursor of CDs during the thermal polymerization can enhance the connection of CDs with g-C3N4. The electrochemical properties of the obtained samples were also tested by LSV in three electrodes. As shown in Fig. S5†, CN/G0.5 also showed the largest current density among the five as-prepared samples under the same potential, which is in accordance with their photocurrent response tests, thus suggesting that the in situ fabrication of CDs/g-C3N4 is capable of effectively promoting H2 evolution [46].

The better photocatalytic performance of CN/G0.5 could be mainly explained by enhancing the separation of electron-hole pair and restraining the recombination of electrons and holes in the hybrids. As illustrated in the left bottom of Fig. 11 , CDs were acted as the electron acceptors. Under visible light illumination, the photo-induced electrons excited from g-C3N4 can easily transfer to CDs, which could efficiently enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs, and thus exhibited the better ability of photocatalytic H2 evolution. Furthermore, the connection between CDs and g-C3N4 was also an important aspect for its photocatalytic performance, which could improve the charges transfer ability of CDs/g-C3N4. During the preparation process of g-C3N4/Gx hybrids, glucose and urea were firstly mixed and recrystallized, and the hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl groups of glucose and the amino groups of urea made an even distribution of glucose in the urea matrix. Then in the process of the thermal polymerization of urea and the carbonization of glucose, the close connections between CDs and g-C3N4 were formed. This close connection could enhance the charge transfer and prevent the recombination of electron-hole at their interface. Additionally, during the heating process, the released ammonia gas produced by the thermal polymerization of urea could produce a protective gas to prevent the CDs from burning. As a result, the CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with uniform CDs distribution and close interface connection could be easily prepared by this method.

In summary, CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids were successfully synthesized by a facile method using urea and glucose as the precursors, which could enhance the connection between CDs and g-C3N4 after thermal polymerization because of the hydrogen bonds formed between their precursors. The prepared CN/G0.5 hybrids exhibited superior stability and the best HER (2.34 mmol g−1 h−1), which is 4.55 times of that of BCN (0.51 mmol g−1 h−1). According to the photoluminescence spectra, photoelectrochemical test and linear scan voltammograms, the improved photocatalytic activity could be ascribed to the enhancements of electron-hole pair separation and the restraints of the electrons and holes recombination. Furthermore, the tight connection of CDs/g-C3N4 at the interface could serve as a well electron transport channel, which may be helpful in improving their photocatalytic activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Program for Science ＆ Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 15HASTIT008), Henan province science and technology open cooperation project (No. 2017-60) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21274133).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.11.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based photocatalysts for artificial photosynthesis and environmental remediation: are we a step closer to achieving sustainability?
